Fantastic work overall! Your tests are great and your code was easy to follow. As mentioned earlier, I do have a few stylistic critiques and suggestions to make the logic more robust.

Also two general comments:

- Should we emit a message telling the user what we inferred the target version to be as part of `--verbose`? 
- By inferring a default for `--target-version`, Black will never do per-file auto-detection if `project.requires-python` exists and is valid. This is certainly better than the status quo for the lower bound, but it can cause some parsing trouble if the file in question is actually using more modern syntax than specified.[^1]

  Say for example we have `requires-python = ">=3.7"`. If I format a file that's using 3.10's new match statement, Black will fail with a `Cannot parse: ...` error because [Black decides what grammars it'll try from the target version](https://github.com/psf/black/blob/f16333e78ba77ab29ab0b75e10891e6c65c6da7e/src/black/parsing.py#L49-L79). While this keeps things consistent, I don't think it makes for a good user experience. The main two ways to fix this are either to infer a list of all supported target versions or [actually implement this suggestion to make `--target-version` a lower-bound by default](https://github.com/psf/black/issues/751#issuecomment-1168882896). The latter is the long-term fix, but I'm not sure when that will be implemented so the former might be necessary as a temporary fix.[^2]

Thanks again for waiting so patiently <3

[^1]: Also Black would never introduce modern syntax that's not permissible under the project's inferred target version even if the file in question is using more modern syntax -- which is probably fine given explicitly providing `--target-version` works the same way.
[^2]: Unless we decide this is an acceptable failure case and the workaround of explicitly setting `--target-version` is okay -- which is fine by me

I have a few minor comments inline and only reviewed `LFW_People`, since most of the comments also apply to `LFW_Pairs`. Apart from that I have three more comments:

1. The two datasets have a very similar structure. Since they return different things, I think having two separate classes is fine to avoid polymorphism. Still, I wonder if it makes sense to have a common superclass, e.g. `_LFW` that groups the common behavior such as downloads.

The main challenge is to provide "fake data", i.e. the (extracted) data that would be present after `.download()` is completed. It should resemble the structure of the original dataset, but only provide a few dummy images. Let me know if you need help with that.
3. I think we're making some assumptions in this diff:
- GetLogdirSubdirectories is always called (e.g. by plugin_event_accumulator) before ListDirectoryAbsolute is called on the lower level dir (e.g. by directory_watcher). Because the cache built by the former will be used by that latter
- GetLogdirSubdirectories is called on the highest top level dir once, because we'll clear cache every time
- Our implementation of listdir_bfs will be just as or more efficient than gfile.walk, since we're now implementing walk explicitly instead of relying on the walk implementation of each filesystem
- Without a TTL for the cache, we're no longer supporting watching of new files as they're created after TB initial load

I think we need much fewer change, if we simply build a look aside cache with TTL inside ListDirectoryAbsolute, without touching GetLogdirSubdirectories?

Would love to hear thoughts from TB team.

This will need some detail added to the deployment configuration:
 * how to set up the buckets (including any necessary policy)
 * how to acquire the credentials
 * how to configure the backend

I think it makes sense to configure a GCS bucket with `{backendType: "aws", ..}` as it's pretty honest about what's happening with the compatibility layer.  If we eventually need to implement dedicated functionality, we can add a `gcs` backendType without any compatibility worries.

As for testing, the comment mentions helper methods for creating buckets.  Creating buckets as part of testing has gotten us in trouble before, as it's often not an immediate operation (Azure) and there are low limits on the number of buckets allowed (S3).  For the AWS backend, I used our existing buckets, with comments indicating how they were set up, and with credentials in [the secret](https://community-tc.services.mozilla.com/secrets/project%2Ftaskcluster%2Ftesting%2Ftaskcluster-object).  The key is to use unique object names so that parallel test runs don't interfere with one another.  I suspect a similar approach could work for GCS.  Also of note, I only use one (public) bucket for AWS, having verified that S3 checks URL signatures even for public buckets.  Assuming GCS has the same behavior, that might reduce the setup burden.  I think if you create the bucket in the `taskcluster-dev` project then we can all have access to it without any further setup.

Hey thanks for the PR! I know the rest of my review is quite negative, but I genuinely appreciate the time and energy you've put into putting this together!

I don't like this change because it provides the wrong feedback to a not an insignificant group of users. If I (as a Python 3 only user) give Black an invalid file, the Python 3.X parse attempts will fail and then Black exits with a "Black attempted to parse python2 code, [...] please install typed_ast" error... which makes no sense to me as a Python 3 only user. Not only is this hiding the true problem: the input file isn't valid Python 3 code, the confused user, might try the advice to install `typed_ast` which 1) might not work or be a pain, and 2) doesn't actually the fix the issue.

Personally I'm open to pushing most if not all of the disruption to the users still using Black's Python 2 support. My idea would be that Black would need `--target-version py27` (configured automatically via magic or configured manually) to even attempt Python 2 parsing. Although this falls apart when Black is formatting multiple files at once (since someone is bound to have a mix of Python 2 and 3 files... of which our target-version logic can't really describe).

There are couple of other configs that I highly recommend to be enabled on a BR  (or router) style device/products:
- For cache table
  -  Suggest increasing `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_TMF_ADDRESS_CACHE_MAX_SNOOP_ENTRIES`  as well (helps allow more of the 128 to be used for snoop optimization)
  - Also the default value for `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_TMF_ADDRESS_QUERY_MAX_RETRY_DELAY`  is rather long (8 hours which I think is given in spec). We typically used much shorter values in projects/products.
 

Other feature to enable on routers/BRs:
- `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_SEND_LINK_REQUEST_ON_ADV_TIMEOUT` (this helps make the links between router a lot more reliable.
- I also highly recommend enabling child supervision on router (and on SEDs), `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CHILD_SUPERVISION_ENABLE`. 
- And increasing number of buffers (or heap size if heap is being used for msg buffers).

On SED type devices, I recommend:
- `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_INFORM_PREVIOUS_PARENT_ON_REATTACH`
- `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_ATTACH_BACKOFF_ENABLE` (which enabled by default).

A few additional thoughts besides the comments in the review.
About registration of functions, it is not unusual in some web contexts (for example routing in [Flask](https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/1.1.x/quickstart/#routing)).
Usually this permits to have more control on the functions to be exposed, compared to using `all` and separate modules.

When comparing decorators and a mapping dictionary, in this context I prefer decorators because it looks cleaner to me. Moreover, using a `feature` decorator allows to enforce some validation, or other pre or post processing, in a similar way to what it would be possible subclassing a base abstract class.

Anyway using decorators is a personal preference, and some web frameworks allow to use either approach. For example in [aiohttp](https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/web_quickstart.html#run-a-simple-web-server) (`app.add_routes([web.get('/', hello)])` vs `@routes.get('/')`)

I’m okay with a ~1% overhead, especially since your μ±σ confidence
intervals overlap a bunch, and since this feature is clearly important
for non-small datasets.

> (first is unmodified code; second is with this PR):

One trick is to copy the old and new binaries to `./bench-{old,new}`
and then ask `hyperfine` to do a parameter sweep:

```
hyperfine '{tool} --logdir logs' -L tool ./bench-old,./bench-new
```

The main benefit is that it’ll give you a confidence interval around the
relative performance, something like “old is 1.02(±0.06)× faster”.

> I haven't modified the unit tests to cover this behavior because I was a bit unsure about how to go about it. My main thought was parameterizing `reload()` (or `RunLoader` at construction) in order to inject some kind of fake clock object, but I wasn't sure if there was a standard technique used for this. Open to suggestions.

That’s about right. The standard technique often involves doing so with
type parameters such that it’s all optimized out: [`StageReservoir`] and
its `ReservoirControl` do this. As to clock objects specifically: I’ve
not done this, and I don’t know of common patterns, but I imagine that a
simple `trait Clock { fn now(&self) -> Instant }` would suffice here,
and let the test clock own an `Rc<Instant>` or `AtomicI32` or something
that ticks forward each time that it’s called.

I’m okay with omitting dedicated tests for this. It seems unlikely to be
subtly broken, and the failure mode is not catastrophic (read-only, and
the right data still shows up, just later). Feel free to dive in if
you’re interested, but no action requested.

[`StageReservoir`]: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/a906f7191137c1816c0544c00432a308fc627d2f/tensorboard/data/server/reservoir.rs#L70

Mostly LGTM, the big thing that we need resolve IMO is the semi-lazy read behavior. Semi-lazy reading (eagerly reading the first block/file) has been confusing for users, results in redundant reading when there's stage fusion or conversion to a pipeline, and I think we should try to get rid of it when switching lazy execution by default, if possible. I think that it would be much better if we had fully lazy mode and eager mode, where semi-lazy execution is an execution optimization when we determine we only need to compute a subset of blocks; these semantics should translate pretty well to our new execution planner and streaming execution model.

Instead of always computing the first block, I think that we should make the read fully lazy, and if the user immediately calls `ds.schema()` right after reading, we only then trigger reading of the first block (and only if the schema isn't already available from e.g. file metadata).

I _think_ it should be pretty straightforward to move this logic to the `ExecutionPlan`, since we can have `ExecutionPlan.schema()` (and other ops that only require the first block) trigger semi-lazy execution with e.g. a certain flag set, and if that flag is set AND the plan only consists of a read stage AND the input blocks are a lazy block list, we trigger minimal computation of that read stage.

There are some approaches we can take with the "reason". On the "code" key, we choose to send `1000` by default, but that's not quite what is written in the specs. We could have choose to not send it, and the [server would assume `1000`](https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/blob/8b05af307e308e3e188fa2b4b0c4d47112321933/uvicorn/protocols/websockets/wsproto_impl.py#L299).

As for the "reason" key, we can do the same, and assume default being empty string. Or we can just do as the suggested snippets and only send the reason if a reason is added. 

I've just noticed an issue on uvicorn on the way [we retrieve the reason](https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/blob/8b05af307e308e3e188fa2b4b0c4d47112321933/uvicorn/protocols/websockets/wsproto_impl.py#L300), as we don't check if reason is None, which is allowed by the spec. I'll be opening a PR there. 

Reference for the new ASGI spec: https://asgi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/specs/www.html#close-send-event

Clarifying the design here:
```
class RLOptimizer:

	def configure_optimizers():
		pass

	def compute_loss():
		pass

	def on_before_compute_loss():
		pass

	def on_after_compute_loss():
		pass

###### NOTE: this is not in the docstring format, please modify it to a more concise form for the purpose of the docstring.
 Please also don't read too much in the name of attributes and methods that I picked here. We can finalize the names once 
 the functionality is fully fleshed out. 
 

###### also note that all this code is getting run and constructed on a single process

class RLTrainerActor:

	def __init__():
		# the single/multi agent Module that is constructed inside the RLTrainer
		self.module = ...
		# the module is passed by reference to the optimizer
		self.rl_optimizer = RLOptimizer(self.module, ...)
		# an alias to the optimizers object created after we invoke this method
		self.optimizers = self.rl_optimizer.configure_optimizers()

	def update(fwd_in, **kwargs):
		fwd_in: SampleBatchType = ...

		# if module is MARLModule sample will be MABatch and the output will also be in MA format
		fwd_out = self.module.forward_train(fwd_in=sample)
		###### ----> This is where the on_before_compute_loss() could go
		loss_out = self.rl_optimizer.compute_loss(fwd_in, fwd_out)
		###### ----> This is where on_after_compute_loss() could go and so on. 
		self.rl_optimizer.log_and_compile(fwd_in, fwd_out, loss_out)
		grads = self.compute_gradients(loss_out)
		self.apply_gradients(grads)

	def compute_gradients(loss_out):
		# for torch
		loss_out["loss_total"].backward()
		grads = {n: p.grad for n, p in self.module.named_parameters()}
		return grads

	def apply_gradients(grads):
		# for torch
		self.optimizers.step()

# the RLTrainerActor will itself be managed by the TrainerRunner which will coordinate the RLTrainerActors and which 
module they will be responsible for. But RLTrainerActor's code does not care whether the underlying module is a 
MARLModule or RLModule or whether the rl_optimizer is RLOptimizer or MARLOptimizer. The API and functionality looks the 
same. 


```

Thanks a lot for this PR, really appreciated!!! I see a great potential for this idea, but I do have some thoughts and ideas.

If, however, it would be possible to really merge the graphs (e.g. reduce/remove the space between the graph, only ticks and labels on the lower x-axis to save space, synchronised x-axes such that zooming/shifting in one axis works for all axes) I would see a tremendous advantage! (The holy grail in this sense I think would be to have the option to add or remove graphs on the go, but this might be (I don't know) extremely difficult).
This might be achievable using the  pyqtgraph.GraphicsLayoutWidget or the pyqtgraph.MultiPlotItem.

I do realize that this might be a bit (maybe a lot) more difficult than the current implementation, so I'm not sure if this is the right way to go, but just wanted to write down my thoughts on this.

If we have to deal with long statements including the new format strings, it also gets a bit more complicated to split them over multiple lines.
I would use implicit string merging within parentheses in those cases, the only downside is that every partial string has to be prefixed with an f:
```
(f'This is a {foo} example'
 f'of a {bar} multiline format string.')
```

> the only workaround I found was compiling the expression and using a regex flag there

I think this feature makes sense nonetheless, but know that you can define flags in the pattern itself:

```python
>>> from parsel import Selector
>>> text = """
... <script>
... function example() {
... 
KeyboardInterrupt
>>> text = """
... <script>
...     function example() {
...         "name": "Adrian",
...         "points": 3,
...     }
... </script>
... """
>>> sel = Selector(text=text)
>>> sel.css('script').re_first(r"(?s)example\(\) ({.*})")
'{\n        "name": "Adrian",\n        "points": 3,\n    }'
```
